words: SAM SCHRAGER: When you started to work in the woods-
WILLIAM BURKLAND: Well, I started in the woods when I was a kid, but that was at
home. I worked for Potlatch in '36- no, wait now- 1910. 1910 I worked
for Potlatch; summer of 1910. But before that we worked- yeah, we wor
ked at home, that is, logged to Dahlbergs. So I logged in the woods-
I've worked in the woods all my life.

speaker: SS
words: Well when y°u were young and learning, what did your father have you

speaker: SS
words: do?

speaker: WB
words: Everything. Everything there was to do. Even skid. I drove horses

speaker: WB
words: when I was twelve years old. I started driving horses. From then on

speaker: WB
words: I took over the horses. When I waa around fifteen, sixteen I took over

speaker: WB
words: the horses entirely. My father didn't want to drive horses. So I

speaker: WB
words: got that job, and I liked it so I stayed with it. I drove horses for

speaker: WB
words: over forty years. Even owned horses of my own since Iwas around- well,

speaker: WB
words: in the '20's, I think. And I bought two colts, three year olds- no,

speaker: WB
words: they were younger than that- I bought one year old colts. I bought two

speaker: WB
words: of 'em in 19— When the Fair was in Seattle. The summer when the Fair

speaker: WB
words: was in Seattle. I raised them and started working them when they were

speaker: WB
words: three years old. And I worked them all the time. I logged with them

speaker: WB
words: when they were four years old; I logged with them for a feller down be

speaker: WB
words: low Deary, there. Fellow by the name of Asplund. In fact, he was Philip

speaker: WB
words: Asplund's halfbrother. And he had a contract to log, south of Deary.

speaker: WB
words: And I worked there in 1912 for him; worked all winter, that's the first

speaker: WB
words: winter I logged with that team. I worked in the woods ever since I

speaker: WB
words: remember. Sawed and swamped and skidded and drayed. Used dray logging.

speaker: WB
words: That was down to the mill., I told you we was at Pete Dahlberg's mill.

speaker: WB
words: We logged down there. And I done that as far back as I can remember,

speaker: WB
words: almost.

speaker: SS
words: When you say dray logging; what do you mean by that?

speaker: WB
words: One sled. We'd load a log on one sled, that was dray hauling. Drug

speaker: WB
words: one end of the log behind. They dray hauled. Potlatch - we done that

speaker: WB
words: even with the Potlatch for a long time. They dray hauled in the winter

speaker: WB
words: time and dolly hauled in the summertime. Dolly- they had a two wheel

speaker: WB
words: ed- made the wheels out of logs, and ironed 'em with two-

speaker: SS
words: bands-

speaker: WB
words: Bands around. That was dolly logging. And then they rolled the log

speaker: WB
words: on there and it pretty near balanced it, you see on the bunk. It didn't

speaker: WB
words: drag very hard. You could adjust that the way you wanted it. If it

speaker: WB
words: was steep downhill you could- roll the log so it was heavier behind and

speaker: WB
words: dragging harder and if you wanted it light on the behind, why, you put

speaker: WB
words: the log up ahead and almost balanced it. And they hauled awfully big

speaker: WB
words: loads that way. We'd use horses. They logged that way lots of times

speaker: WB
words: in the summertime. Potlatch did, before they started with the donkeys

speaker: WB
words: and then they started with the donkeys and then they pulled in the

speaker: WB
words: whole tree. That's what I was working on in 1910. I worked on the don

speaker: WB
words: key, and we pulled in whole trees. Of course, I didn't— well, I war

speaker: WB
words: ked out in the woods, too, . And then I worked on the skidway

speaker: WB
words: where they pulled 'em in. They pulled in the whole tree and then we

speaker: WB
words: had to measure it up and saw 'em off by hand. Me and my older brother

speaker: WB
words: and Bill Olson. Bill Olson - Did you know Alfred Olson in Troy?

speaker: SS
words: I knew who he was, yeah.

speaker: WB
words: You knew who he was?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: WB
words: Well, it was his brother, his older brother. He worked with us on the

speaker: WB
words: skidway in 1910. And it was early in the spring and it was wet too;

speaker: WB
words: the logs were wet and the trees were wet; the ground was wet. And the

speaker: WB
words: sun got awfully hot and he went out for us. He was working there and

speaker: WB
words: it got too hot^ he was the kind of a guy that couldn't sweat, and he

speaker: WB
words: went out and we had to drag him out to the shade. But we went ahead

speaker: WB
words: and worked and he laid there about, oh, two, three hours.

speaker: SS
words: Was he a big man?

speaker: WB
words: A big man? No, he wasn't a big man, just ordinary. But he couldn't

speaker: WB
words: sweat. He was one of them hidebound fellers, you know, that couldn't

speaker: WB
words: sweat. And my brother and Lwe sweat till was just running

speaker: WB
words: off of us. It was so hot in there and all that water and the sun was

speaker: WB
words: hot. It was suffocating. But then he got over it after he laid there

speaker: WB
words: a while, he got over it and come up and said he was going to help us

speaker: WB
words: so we put him on to measuring and we took the saw and sawed. And, oh,

speaker: WB
words: I 11 tell you, that wood work was awful hard work.

speaker: SS
words: Was it rough?

speaker: WB
words: Yes. It's hard work. You sawed by hand, you know. You stand there

speaker: WB
words: and worked all day; that was hard work.

speaker: SS
words: I'll bet there was a lot of it, too.

speaker: WB
words: Ya, you ain't a kiddin', there was a lot of it. There was about- well,

speaker: WB
words: when they logged there was about two gangs to the team, to keep a team

speaker: WB
words: going if they didn't have too awfully far to skid where it took two

speaker: WB
words: gangs to keep them agoing.

speaker: SS
words: How many men was in a gang?

speaker: WB
words: Well, you had two men to the gang.

speaker: SS
words: Two sawyers?

speaker: WB
words: Ya. Two sawyers. One on each end of the saw. And then there was two

speaker: WB
words: of them. Two saws to keep a team going, unless it was so far skidding.

speaker: WB
words: But they didn't have- they didn't skid very far when they skidded out

speaker: WB
words: to the— see they're on trackside, spurs, railroad spurs, and they

speaker: WB
words: didn't figure on, oh, on, eighty rods, something like that.

speaker: SS
words: Were they using dollies then with—?

speaker: WB
words: No. Not when they skidded that way. The dollies, they were when they

speaker: WB
words: skidded further than that. They skidded a quarter of a mile or a

speaker: WB
words: half a mile, then they used dollies. But that was in the summertime

speaker: WB
words: when it was dry. But they did use drays even when it was wet, it didn't

speaker: WB
words: have to be snow, if the ground was wet they used drays and they put the

speaker: WB
words: log on a dray and balanced it and pull it in that way. That was the

speaker: WB
words: way they logged before they got the donkeys and the cats. Well, the

speaker: WB
words: cats, they didn't get them until way late.

speaker: SS
words: Were you working by the day? You got so much a day?

speaker: WB
words: Yeah. That wasn't too much, about two dollars. There wasn't much money

speaker: WB
words: in it then, them days, you know. We didn't have any money. I worked in

speaker: WB
words: the harvest field, not in the harvest field, on the threshing machine

speaker: WB
words: for thirty-five days, straight through driving derrick team for two

speaker: WB
words: dollars a day. That was sixteen hours a day, too. (Chuckles) We was

speaker: WB
words: up at four o'clock in the morning and harnessed up and lots of evenings

speaker: WB
words: we had to work in— well, we didn't work in lamplight, but the sewers

speaker: WB
words: did. They had to have a lantern for them to see to sew in the evening.

speaker: SS
words: Sack sewers?

speaker: WB
words: Sack sewers. It was all sacked grain then. And the sack sewers had to

speaker: WB
words: work in lamplight and of course, we didn't need that. The horses they

speaker: WB
words: knew the track they'd been going there all day long; forwards and

speaker: WB
words: backwards and forwards and backwards. We were pulling headed grain up

speaker: WB
words: on a derrick and then there was the feeder run back and there was two

speaker: WB
words: men on there raking that into the feeder, getting it into the machine.

speaker: WB
words: Them days the threshing machines had about, oh, let's see, two, four,

speaker: WB
words: six- twelve, fourteen men on the crew; on the machine crew.

speaker: SS
words: You were driving team?

speaker: WB
words: I was driving team. I was driving the derrick team.

speaker: SS
words: What did the derrick team do?

speaker: WB
words: They pulled in the headed grain from the stack. You know what a derrick

speaker: WB
words: fork is?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, I've seen a picture of 'em.

speaker: WB
words: Well, they pulled them back on the stack and put them down in that

speaker: WB
words: headed grain, and then they motioned us to go ahead and we pulled up

speaker: WB
words: that load and and it took em up in the mast and it tripped it and it

speaker: WB
words: come down on the great big platform- oh, it was half as big as this room.

speaker: WB
words: That's where they dropped it down and there was a man on each side ra

speaker: WB
words: king that in- into the- what did I call it? elevator? And then it went

speaker: WB
words: into the machine.

speaker: SS
words: How did they decide whether they were going to thresh it all in a b ig

speaker: SS
words: pile like that or whether they'd put it in bundles?

speaker: WB
words: Well, they were out there with a header, the header bed, and then they

speaker: WB
words: had a header bed- that was made something like a bundle tack only much

speaker: WB
words: bigger. And then they hauled it into the stack, put it in there, just

speaker: WB
words: like a stack, you know. You open a stack and you thresh it all. If

speaker: WB
words: they wanted any divisions why they'd have to divide it before they come

speaker: WB
words: to the stack with it.

speaker: SS
words: Divisions?

speaker: WB
words: Yeah, if there was any division in the grain to be done, it had to be

speaker: WB
words: divided before they come to the stack with it. And after it was in the

speaker: WB
words: stack then it went in the machine. Same with all of it. Same way with

speaker: WB
words: bundles. You know, they stacked bundles, too. And after they started

speaker: WB
words: bundle threshing. But now in the Palouse country down around Colton

speaker: WB
words: and Uniontown and down in there it was all headed grain.

speaker: SS
words: Is that where you went to work?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, that's where I went to work. That's where I worked. Out of Gene

speaker: SS
words: see. Yeah, I worked out to Uniontown, west of Genesee. Started on the

speaker: SS
words: Didrick Sonhaas place. You know where that is?

speaker: SS
words: No, but I know a little bit of the country. I don't know that parti

speaker: SS
words: cular place. Big place?

speaker: SS
words: Oh, ya, well, they were all The Didrick Sonhaas place that's

speaker: SS
words: right on the edge of Genesee. That's not a very big- that's a 160 acres.

speaker: SS
words: And then it was Charlie Sonaas and Fred Sonhaas and fluramers, they were

speaker: SS
words: all related. Fred and Charlie Sonhaas and Dick, they were brothers.

speaker: SS
words: And then there's a Flummer, he was married to Sonhaas's girl. And then

speaker: SS
words: it was, oh, I don't remember the names of all them through there.

speaker: SS
words: Did you enjoy harvest?

speaker: SS
words: Yes. I liked it. Slept out in the strawstack. Oh, yeah, I liked that!

speaker: SS
words: You get so you like some of them things, you know. Sleeping out in the

speaker: SS
words: straw pile and getting up at four. The engineer was up before thatwell,

speaker: SS
words: he got up before that so he had a whistle and he whistled and geeminy,

speaker: SS
words: you had to roll out. It hurt your ears! (Chuckles) Yes, I liked

speaker: SS
words: that, them days, I wouldn't like it now! In them days I liked it.

speaker: SS
words: Did the men work really hard on something like that?

speaker: SS
words: Hard as they wanted to. There was really no driving unless - if you

speaker: SS
words: was pitching bundles, that was harder than the rest of the work around

speaker: SS
words: there, pitching bundles. Then you had to pitch in order to beat every

speaker: SS
words: thing in order to get that into the machine and get out in the field

speaker: SS
words: again. And some of them bundles were both long and heavy. So that was

speaker: SS
words: really harder work. The header- or the derrick team work that was easy

speaker: SS
words: work, but I was just a kid then; I was about sixteen years old. So that

speaker: SS
words: wasn't hard work, but you had to be there all day right in the dust

speaker: SS
words: back of them teams. Backward and forward and backward and forward,

speaker: SS
words: and you went one time and the other feller went one time. And you

speaker: SS
words: come back and your forker. he took his fork and the other one he took

speaker: SS
words: his. Two teams and two forkers. You see- there was an opening in be

speaker: SS
words: tween the stacks, and one built up the stacks from that way and the

speaker: SS
words: other one built it up from this way back. Well, the stack was started

speaker: SS
words: from the dividing line, and then they built 'em up so they were out

speaker: SS
words: there about forty or fifty feet, each side, and that's where we had to

speaker: SS
words: pull 'em in there after that.

speaker: SS
words: That's big sized stacks.

speaker: WB
words: Oh, yeah, big stacks there, you bet. They had big stacks, they were

speaker: WB
words: about, oh, eight or ten feet high and they must have been about eight

speaker: WB
words: or ten feet wide. Oh, I tell you, that was lots of grain. T hey had

speaker: WB
words: lots of grain out there at Genesee and Uniontown they were pretty big

speaker: WB
words: farms, some of 'em were big farms. And even 160 acres, you know, the

speaker: WB
words: way wheat grows out there them years, I think they grew more wheat than

speaker: WB
words: they do now. More straw, anyway. And that made a lot of straw. Your

speaker: WB
words: header cut down- the straw was about that high- the header, you know,

speaker: WB
words: it cut up pretty high on the straw, but then they left about that much.

speaker: WB
words: To get all the heads they had to leave about a foot and a half, something

speaker: WB
words: like that, sixteen inches, and that all went into the stack.

speaker: SS
words: So that country around Genesee seemed pretty prosperous to you in those

speaker: SS
words: days?

speaker: WB
words: Yes, yes. That was always good country. That was the queen of the country,

speaker: WB
words: in Genesee. They claim there was some that was better than

speaker: WB
words: Genesee, but, I tell you, that was awfully hard to beat between Genesee

speaker: WB
words: and Uniontown. That was an awful fertile country.

speaker: SS
words: Did lots of guys come from over here to work over there

speaker: SS
words: Oh, yeah. Yes, pretty near all the old-timers worked over there.

speaker: SS
words: They worked over there in the harvest and a lot of 'em now like Arthur

speaker: SS
words: dad, he worked there, oh, there for several years before my time,

speaker: SS
words: He worked there from the time he came. He came out to Genesee and he

speaker: SS
words: worked around there for a long time, both in the harvest and the spring

speaker: SS
words: work. People were out there in the spring work, too. You know, they

speaker: SS
words: worked a lot of men^them days. It was all horse work and it was only

speaker: SS
words: about, oh, all the way from four to six horses for every teamster.

speaker: SS
words: And it wasn't very many teamsters that used six horses, they used three

speaker: SS
words: and four and they had to have a man with every team. There was a lot

speaker: SS
words: of man work them days. That's been done away and they're doing away

speaker: SS
words: with it right along. The man labor.

speaker: SS
words: Who was the outfit that you— would you go work for the same outfit

speaker: SS
words: when you'd go over there?

speaker: SS
words: I worked for several outfits over there. This time when I was driving

speaker: SS
words: derrick team, I worked for Nels Johnson. He lived right out south of

speaker: SS
words: town here.

speaker: SS
words: He took his whole operation from here over there?

speaker: WB
words: Ya, a lot of 'em anyway. You mean his outfit?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: WB
words: He kePt his outfit- he bought itK Genesee, he bought it from Dick

speaker: WB
words: Sonhaas. And he always left it there when he worked it there. He was

speaker: WB
words: down there about three or four years, I think, and he kept his machinery

speaker: WB
words: down here. And then I worked for John Vedwick. He was from Troy. I

speaker: WB
words: worked for him after that. That was a bundlerrig, though. And I wor

speaker: WB
words: ked for Halvorson. Halvorson and Johnson down here. Worked for them,

speaker: WB
words: in the threshing. That was also a bundlerrig. Well, that was three

speaker: WB
words: years. There wasn't much more than that. I worked for- I was out there

speaker: WB
words: and hauled grain one fall; helped haul grain, helped August Johnson.

speaker: WB
words: He a Genesee man, he lived this side of Genesee.

speaker: SS
words: So one of these crews, one of these outfits with all the men on itwould

speaker: SS
words: quite a few of the men in the outfit be Troy area men, or the

speaker: SS
words: Deary area?

speaker: WB
words: It was with Vedwick, there was a lot of them. That whole crew was from

speaker: WB
words: Troy. And that was Oslund- that was John Oslund and Pete Swenson and

speaker: WB
words: Charlie Swanburg and a fellow by the name of Alfred Westlund and John

speaker: WB
words: Estlund. I don't know if I can remember any more.

speaker: SS
words: I'm surprised that you can remember so many that you do.

speaker: WB
words: Well, they were from Troy, they were all from Troy. And Andrew Heidahl;

speaker: WB
words: I drove Heidahl's team. I hauled bundles.

speaker: SS
words: Well, how much of the year, let's say around 1910, how much of the year

speaker: SS
words: could you work out in the woods or in the harvest? How much work was

speaker: SS
words: there?

speaker: WB
words: Oh, about thirty days.

speaker: SS
words: For harvest?

speaker: WB
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: What about woods work? How long could you work there?

speaker: WB
words: Oh, that's the year around, you know, the woodswork, that was the year

speaker: WB
words: around, if you wanted to be there all the time. The lumberjacks, that

speaker: WB
words: was the year around for them, sometimes. In some camps. Well, pretty

speaker: WB
words: near all the camps at that time when they used horses.. They didn't

speaker: WB
words: lay up for the winter, they wanted that snow. Snow and cold weather

speaker: WB
words: that's what the loggers wanted.

speaker: SS
words: So that means that those days, you could work pretty near as much as

speaker: SS
words: you wanted to.

speaker: WB
words: Yes. Work as much as you wanted to; pretty near had to to made it.

speaker: SS
words: Were you working the year around, yourself? Around 1910?

speaker: WB
words: Well, no. No, I was there in the summertime in 1910. I was there one

speaker: WB
words: year was all. One summer. ButA worked at home, that's where we wor

speaker: WB
words: ked in the wintertime. We stayed home and logged down here at the mill,

speaker: WB
words: Dahlberg's mill.

speaker: SS
words: What were you doing on the place then? You were logging? Mainly, or

speaker: SS
words: was that just winter?

speaker: WB
words: That was in the wintertime, we didn't log in the summertime.

speaker: SS
words: What else did you do on the place?

speaker: WB
words: Oh, we raised hay and grain and cleared land and made wood and I hauled

speaker: WB
words: wood to Troy along when I was about fourteen years old, I started hauling

speaker: WB
words: wood to Troy. We hauled about ten cords of wood to Troy every winter

speaker: WB
words: for so long, you know, I don't know how long. I hauled wood every win

speaker: WB
words: ter to Troy besides logging.

speaker: SS
words: What was that trip like? Hauling the wood from Bear Creek to Troy?

speaker: WB
words: What was it like?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: WB
words: Like any other horse work.

speaker: SS
words: I mean hauling it in. What was the road like?

speaker: WE
words: Oh, the road was good in the wintertime. Oh, ya. The road was real

speaker: WE
words: good in the wintertime. They didn't do the roads then like they do now.

speaker: WE
words: The horses was the ones that made the roads. They tramped up the trail

speaker: WE
words: and it wasn't icy like it is now. Now they scrape it, you know scrape

speaker: WE
words: the snow off and it freezes ice. They go down to where there's moisture

speaker: WE
words: and probably below that and then it freezes at night and you get just a

speaker: WE
words: glare of black ice in some places. It wasn't that way then. The road

speaker: WE
words: was tramped down by horses and it was usually a real nice road and your

speaker: WE
words: sled would run just as light as you please.

speaker: SS
words: How much could you haul in one load?

speaker: WB
words: Well, it all depended on how big a team you had. We had small horses.

speaker: WB
words: I hauled about three quarters of a cord up to a cord, around a cord if

speaker: WB
words: it was nice, dry wood, why, we hauled a cord to the load.TTw^'5 ^oh^'t a

speaker: SS
words: How much were you getting in Troy?

speaker: WB
words: I suppose it was around three dollars, I think. Three dollars a cord.

speaker: SS
words: Was that a day's work to get down there and back?

speaker: WB
words: Yes, that was a day's work. It was a long day's, too. Not too long

speaker: WB
words: about a ten hour day. Eight or nine hours anyway, it all depend^ on how

speaker: WB
words: good a team you had to walk. What the team could walk. I never run

speaker: WB
words: my horses. I walked 'em. And that way they stood it every day, I could

speaker: WB
words: go every day. If you'd run your horses, you'd play 'em out, but I never

speaker: WB
words: did that, I walked my horses. I had small horses and they didn't make

speaker: WB
words: as good a time as bigger horses o\oL5.

speaker: SS
words: When you say smaller, about what would they weigh?

speaker: WB
words: Eleven hundred and twelve; not over twelve.

speaker: SS
words: How many did you have on a team? Two?

speaker: WB
words: Oh, I had just two horses- two horse team in the wintertime. Person

speaker: WB
words: didn't like to drive four horses in the wintertime because it was up

speaker: WB
words: and down hill, too much. Some places you have to rough-lock, you know

speaker: WB
words: to go down with a load.

speaker: SS
words: How did you rough-lock 'em?

speaker: WB
words: Well, you had a rough-lock made and you had what they call a rough-lock

speaker: WB
words: lock on your chain. You had a chain you took under your runner and then

speaker: WB
words: you run it back here and you had a lock, you had a double piece of iron

speaker: WB
words: that you run your chain through the link, then you had a ring up here

speaker: WB
words: that you dropped down, bear down on that, when you got to the bottom

speaker: WB
words: you went over there and kicked that ring off and went on. Your roughlock,

speaker: WB
words: it was on the- either the rack or the sleigh bar, on your sledge.

speaker: WB
words: When you kicked that off it was free. You take the Troy hill, now, we

speaker: WB
words: had to rough-lock that all the way down. We come in on the north side

speaker: WB
words: at Troy, and come up on top for that road. And that was - at one time

speaker: WB
words: it was steeper than it is now by a long shot. They've been grading that

speaker: WB
words: for over eighty years! They've got it pretty good now. Them days, you

speaker: WB
words: know, you had pitches there to ride down. And, doggone it, I had a run

speaker: WB
words: away team^caught up with me down there.A It hit my rig. I was hauling

speaker: WB
words: lumber that time, and the one horse started up my tieboard, you know,

speaker: WB
words: when you haul lumber you had a tieboard, you put a twist on about the

speaker: WB
words: middle of your fcobs and then you lean that- let that tieboard hang

speaker: WB
words: back and it was getting slick by the time I got down to Troy and it

speaker: WB
words: was laying right down on the load and it bent down pretty near to the

speaker: WB
words: ground. This runaway team caught up with me and one of them horses

speaker: WB
words: run up that board and come up on the load and jumped off- (Laughter)

speaker: WB
words: I had to run my team off the road and out in the snow and they went on.

speaker: WB
words: But one of them horses got killed though, just the same. He was the

speaker: WB
words: one that run up the board, but the other horse pushed him into my load

speaker: WB
words: and he hit his shoulder against the load of lumber before he ever

speaker: WB

words: I don't know. They didn't sell that, they fed it to the pigs and the
